images.caption: Figure 1:Implicit transcription emerges without speech-recognition supervision.Logit-lens analysis of intermediate states for the spoken prompt “The capital of the United Kingdom is…”. Cells show textual tokens probability, from light yellow for zero to dark blue for high probability. The labels show the most probable relevant textual token at each position; notably, the model predicts “London” although it was not spoken in the prompt.

Figure 2:Speech LMs operate in text.
Modality distribution of inner state Logit Lens. (a) Sum of probabilities over the all speech tokens and text tokens respectively. (b) Same but only considering top 200 tokens.

Figure 3:Implicit transcription and textual continuation emerge in speech hidden states.We apply the logit lens to speech-token hidden states and reportRecall​@​k\mathrm{Recall@}kup to a given layer, for the current transcription word, the next word, and the final answer. Although the models are not explicitly trained for transcription, current-word transcription emerges reliably in intermediate layers across models, while next-word and answer-word predictions are weaker but still decodable. The random-token baseline remains near zero.

Figure 4:Implicit transcription ability is positively correlated with factual knowledge retrieval.Each point represents a model. Thexx-axis reports the percentage of words for which the correct current-word transcription (left) or next-word transcription (right) appears in the top-1010logit-lens predictions at any aligned speech-token position and layer. Theyy-axis shows the binary accuracy on our commonsense factual-knowledge benchmark. Both transcription scores correlates positively with knowledge scores.
Figure 5:Log Likelihood based evaluations for all models we used

Figure 6:Implicit transcription and textual continuation emerge in speech hidden states for text pre-trained with interleaving.We apply the logit lens to speech-token hidden states and report Recall@k up to a given layer, for the current transcription word, the next word, and the final answer. Although the models are not explicitly trained for transcription, current-word
transcription emerges reliably in intermediate layers across models, while next-word and answer-word predictions
are weaker but still decodable. The random-token baseline remains near zero.

Figure 7:Logit lens of intermediate states for the spoken input "lime", using the Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (official) model. The example shows both gradual transcription, with an early prediction of
"lie", and a transcription error, where the model predicts "line" instead of "lime".

Figure 8:Logit lens of intermediate states for the spoken input "white", using the Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (official) model. The example shows gradual transcription, where the model first predicts the partial form "why" before later converging to the full word "white".

Figure 9:Logit lens of intermediate states for the spoken input "Pakistan", using the Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (official) model. The example shows gradual transcription, where the model first predicts the partial form "pack” and later updates toward later components of the word, such as "istan", before converging to "Pakistan".

Figure 10:Logit lens of intermediate states for the spoken input "teacher", using the Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (official) model. The example shows gradual transcription, where the model first predicts "tea", then "teach", and finally converges to the full word "teacher".

Figure 11:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "The capital of United Kingdom is…", using Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (official).This version contains less filters on the time and layer domain and thus show higher resolution in the inner mechanism by logit lens perspective
Figure 12:Logit lens of inner states of the spoken input: "Paris is the capital of …", using Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (official).
Figure 13:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "Paris is the capital of …", using Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (ours).
Figure 14:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "Paris is the capital of …", using Llama-3.2 PI-2/3.
Figure 15:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "Paris is the capital of…", using Qwen2.5-3B PI-1/3 60k
Figure 16:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "Paris is the capital of…", using Qwen2.5-1.5B PI-1/3 42K
Figure 17:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "The capital of France is…", using Llama-3.2 PI-1/3 (official).
Figure 18:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "The capital of France is…", using Llama-3.2 PI-2/3
Figure 19:Logit Lens of inner states of the spoken input: "The capital of France is…", using Qwen2.5-3B PI-1/3 60k.
